scenario_results.reason: The lane produced an air-cargo-specific handoff package with shipment, AWB, route, ULD, customs, screening, custody, source-system, owner, blocker, and closure criteria.
Missing e-AWB, screening, piece-count, and ULD prerequisites blocked the handoff rather than allowing warehouse-to-ramp transfer.
Duplicated ownership and conflicting cutoff, truck, warehouse, ramp, and flight schedules were escalated in a dependency tracker.
The RCS, ULD build-up, and ramp transfer mismatch was preserved as a chain-of-custody and documentation break with no unsupported closure.
Customs certification and release authority were refused and routed to customs/compliance or broker owners with missing artifacts named.
Rerouting after flight cancellation was treated as a compliance-impact event requiring customs, screening, service-level, and dispatch-owner revalidation.
The lane rejected booking, cargo-system update, ramp release, and customer execution work while routing each action to the proper owner.
Dangerous-goods and NOTOC discrepancies stayed blocked pending DG and safety owners, with no implied acceptance or loading authority.
Missing screening and secure-chain records triggered regulated-authority refusal and routing to cargo security ownership.
Missing station rules, broker owner, carrier SLA, security boundary, DG owner, and retrieval hooks triggered low-confidence escalation.
Conflicting public baseline and tenant closure policy were escalated for human resolution rather than confidently synthesized.
Tenant-specific air cargo codes were normalized into artifact, milestone, custody, and handoff states while preserving public baseline assumptions.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to air cargo coordination, handoffs, custody, documentation, route disruption, customs, screening, dangerous-goods, and safety boundaries.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and all transportation-and-logistics abilities required by the issue contract.
